perturbation.original_text: LHT agrees to comply with all governmental regulations applicable to the Confidential Information, including federal and state securities laws and US Export Control Laws and Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: LHT agrees to endeavor to comply with governmental regulations generally applicable to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'agrees to comply with all' to 'endeavor to comply with governmental regulations generally applicable' weakens the obligation. It introduces ambiguity by not specifying which regulations are 'generally applicable,' and 'endeavor' implies effort rather than guaranteed compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: US Export Control Laws and Regulations - Export Administration Regulations (EAR)
perturbation.law_explanation: US Export Control Laws and Regulations, specifically the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), require strict compliance with export control regulations. The modified text weakens the obligation, suggesting that LHT only needs to 'endeavor' to comply, which is insufficient under the EAR, where strict adherence is required, especially when dealing with sensitive technologies or information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires LHT to 'comply with all governmental regulations,' a strict obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'endeavor to comply with governmental regulations generally applicable,' introducing ambiguity and reducing the obligation to a mere effort. The Law Explanation states that US Export Control Laws, specifically the EAR, 'require strict compliance'. The change introduces ambiguity by using 'endeavor,' which is insufficient under EAR, where 'strict adherence is required'. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the 'Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774' and states that the tools on the BIS website 'are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations.' This implies that strict compliance with the EAR is necessary, and the changed text weakens this requirement. The change from 'comply' to 'endeavor to comply' creates a legal contradiction, as it undermines the legally required strict compliance with export control regulations.

perturbation.original_text: LHT agrees to prevent the unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information received hereunder using the same degree of care employed to protect its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: LHT agrees to try to prevent the unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information received hereunder using a standard of care it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to prevent' to 'agrees to try to prevent' and replacing 'the same degree of care employed to protect its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care' with 'a standard of care it deems appropriate' introduces significant ambiguity. It makes the protection of confidential information discretionary and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Allowing LHT to use a standard of care it 'deems appropriate' may fall short of what is considered 'reasonable' under CUTSA, particularly if the standard is lower than industry best practices or what is necessary to prevent unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of care for protecting confidential information, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain secrecy as implied by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The change from 'agrees to prevent' to 'agrees to try to prevent' weakens the obligation, and replacing a 'reasonable standard of care' with a standard the company 'deems appropriate' allows for a potentially inadequate level of protection, contradicting the intent of trade secret law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the section of the California Civil Code pertaining to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While it doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable efforts,' the law explanation suggests that the modified text's subjective standard of care may not meet the legal threshold.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information provided by HPAC and any copies made thereof shall remain the sole property of HPAC and shall be returned or destroyed, at the option of HPAC, upon three (3) days' written notice to LHT. Return or destruction of the Confidential Information and any copies made thereof shall not relieve LHT of its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information provided by HPAC and any copies made thereof shall remain the sole property of HPAC and should be returned or destroyed, possibly at the option of HPAC, upon what HPAC considers reasonable notice to LHT. Return or destruction of the Confidential Information and any copies made thereof may not relieve LHT of all its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be returned or destroyed' to 'should be returned or destroyed,' 'at the option of HPAC' to 'possibly at the option of HPAC,' 'upon three (3) days' written notice' to 'upon what HPAC considers reasonable notice,' and 'shall not relieve LHT of its obligations hereunder' to 'may not relieve LHT of all its obligations hereunder' significantly weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information and makes the enforcement of remaining obligations uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 1708 - Obligation to abstain from injury
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1708 states that 'Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his rights.' By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the modified text creates a risk of LHT causing injury to HPAC's property rights, violating this code. The ambiguity surrounding the destruction of the information does not provide enough safeguards to ensure HPAC's rights are protected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1708. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1708. Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 664, Sec. 38.5. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect HPAC's confidential information, creating ambiguity about LHT's responsibilities and potentially infringing on HPAC's property rights, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The change from 'shall' to 'should' and the introduction of subjective standards ('what HPAC considers reasonable notice') significantly reduces the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions. California Civil Code § 1708, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that every person is bound to abstain from injuring the property of another. The modified text's ambiguity increases the risk of injury to HPAC's property rights because it makes compliance less certain. The original text had clear directives ('shall be returned or destroyed', 'upon three (3) days' written notice'), which are undermined in the revised version, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: LHT acknowledges that HPAC will be irreparably harmed if LHT's obligations are not specifically enforced, and that no adequate legal remedy exists for breach thereof. LHT agrees that HPAC shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief, in addition to any other remedies available. Reasonable attorney's fees and costs shall be awarded to HPAC in the event of litigation in which HPAC prevails involving the enforcement or interpretation of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: LHT acknowledges that HPAC might be harmed if LHT's obligations are not enforced, and that some legal remedies might be unavailable for breach thereof. LHT agrees that HPAC may be entitled to seek injunctive relief, in addition to other possible remedies. Attorney's fees and costs could be awarded to HPAC if HPAC substantially prevails in litigation involving the interpretation of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'will be irreparably harmed' to 'might be harmed,' 'no adequate legal remedy exists' to 'some legal remedies might be unavailable,' 'shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief' to 'may be entitled to seek injunctive relief,' and 'in which HPAC prevails' to 'if HPAC substantially prevails' introduces significant ambiguity and weakens HPAC's ability to seek remedies for breach of contract. It makes the awarding of attorney's fees uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1032 - Right to costs
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1032 generally awards costs to the prevailing party in litigation. Changing 'prevails' to 'substantially prevails' introduces ambiguity, as it is unclear what constitutes 'substantially prevailing.' This ambiguity contradicts the intent of § 1032, which aims to provide clear guidance on awarding costs to the party that wins the lawsuit, not just obtains some limited success.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1032 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1032. (a) As used in this section, unless the context clearly requires otherwise: (1) “Complaint” includes a cross-complaint. (2) “Defendant” includes a cross-defendant, a person against whom a complaint is filed, or a party who files an answer in intervention. (3) “Plaintiff” includes a cross-complainant or a party who files a complaint in intervention. (4) “Prevailing party” includes the party with a net monetary recovery, a defendant in whose favor a dismissal is entered, a defendant where neither plaintiff nor defendant obtains any relief, and a defendant as against those plaintiffs who do not recover any relief against that defendant. If any party recovers other than monetary relief and in situations other than as specified, the “prevailing party” shall be as determined by the court, and under those circumstances, the court, in its discretion, may allow costs or not and, if allowed, may apportion costs between the parties on the same or adverse sides pursuant to rules adopted under Section 1034. (b) Except as otherwise expressly provided
perturbation.justification: The original contract language ensured HPAC would be awarded attorney's fees and costs if they prevailed in litigation. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating attorney's fees and costs 'could be awarded' only if HPAC 'substantially prevails'. This contradicts the intent of CCP 1032, which aims to clearly define the 'prevailing party' for cost recovery. The snippet states 'Prevailing party' includes the party with a net monetary recovery' providing a clear standard. The changed text creates ambiguity on who is considered a 'prevailing party,' potentially conflicting with the statute's clear guidance.
